title: Arnold Diffusion via Normally Hyperbolic Invariant Cylinders and Mather Variational Method, Part I
speaker: Vadim Kaloshin
affiliation: Pennsylvania State University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: In 1964 Arnold constructed an example of instabilities for nearly integrable systems and conjectured that generically this phenomenon takes place. There has been big progress attacking this conjecture in the past decade. Jointly with Ke Zhang we present a new approach to this problem. It is based on a construction of crumpled and flower Normally Hyperbolic Invariant Cylinders. Once existence of these cylinders is shown to construct diffusion we apply Mather variational mechanism. A part of the project is also joint with P. Bernard.

proof_stages.label: Arnold diffusion problem and generic theorems
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up weak versus strong Arnold diffusion for nearly integrable convex Hamiltonians and states two finite-smooth generic results: order-one action drift in any dimension n≥2 and open-set-to-open-set diffusion for n=2.

proof_stages.label: Classical normally hyperbolic cylinder mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Arnold’s a priori unstable example is used to isolate the mechanism that an invariant two-dimensional cylinder in four-dimensional phase space, with stable and unstable directions and persistent normal hyperbolicity, can support order-one drift in the action coordinate.

proof_stages.label: Normal forms and Conley isolating blocks for cylinder construction
proof_stages.summary: The construction of the needed cylinders is reduced, after resonant normal forms, to an abstract Conley isolating-block/graph-transform fixed point argument with cone domination, entry-exit conditions, and enough regularity control despite nonperturbative distortion.

proof_stages.label: Single-resonance crumpled cylinders
proof_stages.summary: For the n=2 kinetic model, a simple resonant line is punctured near finitely many bad holes, and the surviving normally hyperbolic cylinder is shown heuristically to be highly crumpled, with a one-slow-angle/two-fast-angle structure analogous to Arnold’s example.

proof_stages.label: Double-resonance chaos zone
proof_stages.summary: At an intersection of two resonances, a √ε-scale neighborhood becomes a chaotic zone with two slow angles and one fast angle, so the proof must arrange entrance along one resonant channel and exit along another.

proof_stages.label: Kesian and flower cylinders near double resonance
proof_stages.summary: Inside the chaotic zone one constructs normally hyperbolic cylinders with holes and flower-like topology, organized by diffusion directions and equipped with Kesian two-plane tangencies between appropriate stable and unstable geometries.

proof_stages.label: Heteroclinic jumps and concatenation of diffusion paths
proof_stages.summary: The Kesian property forces robust intersections of three-dimensional stable and unstable manifolds, allowing jumps between cylinders; concatenating diffusion along cylinders, jumps across the bad region, and variational/normal-form control implements the Arnold-Mather diffusion game.

examples.name: Low-regularity perturbations of twist maps destroying invariant curves
examples.note: In response to a regularity question, the speaker recalls Herman/Takens-type phenomena where rough perturbations of integrable twist maps can destroy invariant curves, illustrating why the theorem is formulated in a high finite C^r topology rather than C^0 or C^1.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites low-regularity twist-map perturbations that destroy invariant curves and allow travel, without constructing or analyzing such a perturbation.

examples.name: Arnold’s original pendulum-rotor example
examples.note: The explicit a priori unstable Hamiltonian displays an invariant cylinder with stable and unstable directions, from which the speaker extracts the core idea that diffusion along a normally hyperbolic cylinder gives action drift.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the pendulum-rotor equations, plugs in p=q=0 to verify an invariant cylinder, and discusses its stable and unstable directions.

passes.reason: standard a-priori-unstable pendulum–rotor model used to illustrate the invariant-cylinder diffusion mechanism

examples.name: Dominated linear contraction x↦x/2, y↦y/4
examples.note: As an answer to a question about Kesian tangencies, this toy linear dynamics shows why domination forces generic curves or manifolds to become tangent to the weaker/stronger invariant directions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the map x↦x/2, y↦y/4 to reason concretely that iterates squash curves toward the weak/dominant direction and produce tangency.

examples.name: Linear saddle model u'=u, s'=-s
examples.note: This simple saddle is used to make Conley’s entry-exit condition concrete before stating the isolating-block criterion for normally hyperbolic invariant manifolds.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the saddle model u'=u, s'=-s and uses it to explain the inward/outward boundary conditions for a Conley isolating block.

examples.name: Boundary-attached curve under graph transform
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes iterating a curve or surface attached to opposite sides of a block to illustrate why the graph transform preserves projection and converges to an invariant manifold.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces a boundary-attached curve under iteration in the graph-transform picture, explaining how endpoints remain attached while the curve stretches and yields a fixed manifold.
